questions.questionText: The nerve most commonly injured during parotid surgery is:
questions.explanation: The facial nerve traverses the parotid gland and is at risk during parotid surgery. Hypoglossal nerve lies deeper in the neck. Glossopharyngeal nerve is related to stylopharyngeus and pharynx. Lingual nerve is in the infratemporal/submandibular region.

questions.questionText: The palatine tonsil lies in the tonsillar fossa between:
questions.optionC: Tensor veli palatini and levator veli palatini
questions.explanation: The palatine tonsil is situated in the tonsillar fossa between the palatoglossal and palatopharyngeal arches. The constrictors form deeper pharyngeal wall relations. Tensor and levator veli palatini are soft palate muscles. Genioglossus and hyoglossus are tongue muscles.
questions.source: Snell Clinical Anatomy, Head and Neck

questions.questionText: A fracture involving the pterion is dangerous because it may tear the:
questions.explanation: The pterion overlies the anterior branch of the middle meningeal artery; fracture here can cause extradural hematoma. The anterior cerebral artery is intracranial and not the classic vessel under the pterion. Basilar artery lies on the brainstem. Sigmoid sinus is posterior in the skull.
questions.source: Gray’s Anatomy, Skull and Cranial Cavity
